cards.back: A table — a named set of rows, each with the same columns.
A row — one item in a table.
A column — one property every row in a table has.
A column (or set of columns) whose value is unique and never null, used to identify each row of a table.
A column that stores another table's primary key value, creating a link (relationship) between the two tables.
The same fact stored in more than one row (or table), risking contradictions when only some copies are updated.
A bug where changing one fact requires updating it in several rows, and missing one leaves the data inconsistent.
A bug where you cannot record a fact because it depends on unrelated data not existing yet (e.g. no course without a student enrolled).
A bug where deleting one record accidentally destroys an unrelated fact stored only in that row.
Every cell holds a single atomic value (no repeating groups), and the table has a primary key.
The table is in 1NF and every non-key column depends on the **whole** primary key, not just part of it (no partial dependency).
The table is in 2NF and every non-key column depends **directly** on the primary key, not on another non-key column (no transitive dependency).
Chooses which columns to return from a query (`*` means all columns).
Names the table (or joined tables) a query reads from.
Filters rows, keeping only those matching a condition.
Sorts the result rows, `ASC` (default) or `DESC`, by one or more columns.
Adds a new row to a table: `INSERT INTO Table (cols) VALUES (values);`.
Changes existing values in the rows matched by `WHERE` (all rows if `WHERE` is omitted).
Removes the rows matched by `WHERE` (all rows if `WHERE` is omitted).
Combines rows from two tables wherever the `ON` condition matches (typically a foreign key equalling a primary key), producing one output row per matching pair.
